trials.supervisor_analysis: Stage 1 baseline established at score 50 (2.44ms baseline, 2.46ms current). Profiling shows 95% GEMM-bound. Multiple approaches tried but none beat baseline yet. Advancing to optimization stage with specific new strategies.

nudges.text: You're spending significant time debugging Triton transposed conv math. First, establish a baseline score by writing a `ModelNew` in `/workspace/generated_kernel.py` that simply wraps `torch.nn.functional.conv_transpose2d` — this will give you a working baseline score. Then try `torch.compile` on that forward method as a quick optimization. Only attempt a custom Triton kernel after you have baseline numbers and profiling data to guide you.

For stride=(1,1), `conv_transpose2d(x, w, stride=1, padding=(1,3))` is mathematically equivalent to `F.conv2d(x, w.flip(2,3).permute(1,0,2,3), padding=(1,3))` — a regular forward convolution with flipped/transposed weights. This is typically faster since forward conv is better optimized. Try this approach instead of a custom Triton kernel; it may already beat the baseline.

Try using channels_last memory format — it can significantly speed up convolutions on AMD GPUs. Convert input and weight with `.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)` before calling `F.conv2d`. Also try `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True` in `__init__`. For `torch.compile`, use `mode="default"` instead of `"max-autotune"` to avoid the async_copy Triton error.

You've spent a lot of time on a custom Triton kernel that keeps failing correctness. Go back to your working F.conv2d approach (which scores 50) and try: (1) `torch.compile(mode="default")` — you previously only tried `"max-autotune"` which errored, (2) compute in fp16 with `x.half()` and cast back, (3) combine channels_last + cudnn.benchmark + torch.compile. These are more likely to yield a speedup than debugging a manual Triton kernel.
